questions.question: Armistice Day (now Veterans Day/Remembrance Day) marks the end of World War I. At exactly what time did the armistice take effect?
options: 11:00 AM on November 11, 1918 (the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month)
questions.answer: 11:00 AM on November 11, 1918 (the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month)
questions.explanation: The Armistice of Compiègne was signed at 5:45 AM on November 11, 1918, and took effect at 11:00 AM. In the hours between signing and taking effect, fighting continued — approximately 11,000 casualties occurred on the last morning of WWI (comparable to D-Day). Some commanders ordered attacks knowing the armistice was signed. The symbolism of 11/11/11 was intentional — it was chosen by French military commander Ferdinand Foch and the Allied delegation. Over 20 million soldiers and civilians died in WWI total.
questions.memory_hook: 11,000 casualties occurred on the final morning of WWI after the armistice was signed but before it took effect at 11 AM. Some commanders ordered assaults knowing the war would end in hours. The 11/11/11 time was chosen for its symbolic resonance — those final hours of death were entirely avoidable.

questions.question: The Scramble for Africa divided the continent among European powers at the Berlin Conference (1884-85). How many independent African countries existed when the conference began?
options: None — all of Africa was already colonized
2 — Liberia (backed by freed American slaves) and Ethiopia
About 30 African kingdoms maintained formal independence
questions.answer: 2 — Liberia (backed by freed American slaves) and Ethiopia
questions.explanation: At the start of the Scramble for Africa (1880s), roughly 90% of Africa was governed by indigenous states. By 1914, only Liberia (established 1847 as home for freed American slaves, backed by the US) and Ethiopia (which defeated Italy at Adwa in 1896) maintained independence. Ethiopia's military victory over an Italian army is the most notable case of African military resistance to colonization succeeding. Adwa became a powerful symbol for African independence movements in the 20th century.
questions.memory_hook: Ethiopia defeated Italy's colonial army at Adwa (1896) — the first African nation to successfully defeat a European colonial force in a major battle. This victory made Ethiopia a symbol of African resistance and was why Rastafarianism adopted Ethiopian Emperor Haile Selassie as a religious figure.

questions.question: The Ming Dynasty's voyages under Zheng He (1405-1433) were remarkable. How do they compare to Columbus's voyage of 1492?
options: They were roughly equal in scale; Zheng He used 17 ships compared to Columbus's 3

Zheng He commanded massive 'treasure fleets' of 200-300 ships and 27,000 men — Columbus commanded 3 ships and 90 men. China's ships (some reportedly 120m long) dwarfed anything European sailors had at the time
Zheng He's voyages were exploratory like Columbus; he reached Africa before returning home
Columbus's fleet was actually larger, using 15 ships to Zheng He's 5 large junks
questions.answer: Zheng He commanded massive 'treasure fleets' of 200-300 ships and 27,000 men — Columbus commanded 3 ships and 90 men. China's ships (some reportedly 120m long) dwarfed anything European sailors had at the time
questions.explanation: Zheng He's treasure voyages (1405-1433) under the Yongle Emperor covered Southeast Asia, India, the Persian Gulf, and East Africa — reaching further than Columbus and with incomparably larger fleets. The largest Chinese 'treasure ships' (baochuan) reportedly stretched 120-130 meters — compared to Columbus's Santa María at ~20 meters. China then abandoned this maritime ambition in the 1430s due to political and fiscal conservatism. Had China continued, world history might have been dramatically different.
questions.memory_hook: China had ships 6 times bigger than Columbus's — 200 ships, 27,000 men — decades before Columbus's 3 ships and 90 men. Then China chose isolationism and abandoned the seas. That political decision handed maritime dominance to Portugal and Spain and shaped the next 600 years of world history.

questions.question: In tennis, what is a 'bagel' and what famous match is named 'The Battle of the Sexes'?
options: A 'bagel' is winning a set 6-0; the Battle of the Sexes was the 1973 exhibition match between Billie Jean King and Bobby Riggs
A 'bagel' is a tiebreak set; the Battle of the Sexes was a doubles match between male and female champions
A 'bagel' is serving 12 aces in a set; the Battle of the Sexes described the 1980 Borg-McEnroe Wimbledon final
A 'bagel' is losing a set without winning a point; the Battle of the Sexes was a series between men's and women's Wimbledon champions
questions.answer: A 'bagel' is winning a set 6-0; the Battle of the Sexes was the 1973 exhibition match between Billie Jean King and Bobby Riggs
questions.explanation: A 'bagel' is a 6-0 set (the 0 looks like a bagel). The Battle of the Sexes (September 20, 1973): Bobby Riggs, a former Wimbledon champion and self-described 'male chauvinist pig,' challenged Billie Jean King to prove men were superior. King defeated Riggs 6-4, 6-3, 6-3 in front of 30,000 at the Houston Astrodome and 50 million TV viewers. The match became a landmark cultural moment in women's sports. Earlier that year, Riggs had beaten Margaret Court, which prompted King to accept the challenge.
questions.memory_hook: Bobby Riggs defeated Margaret Court easily, claimed tennis proved female inferiority, then lost to Billie Jean King 6-4, 6-3, 6-3 in front of 50 million viewers. King trained as if her life depended on it; Riggs treated it as entertainment. The match's outcome shaped how the media covered women's sports for years.

questions.question: What is the highest scoring game in NBA history?
questions.explanation: The Detroit Pistons defeated the Denver Nuggets 186-184 in three overtimes on December 13, 1983 — the highest-scoring game in NBA history, with a combined 370 points. Both teams ran the 'run and gun' style; Denver's Dan Issel scored 47 points. The game lasted over 3 hours. For context, the NBA average score in 2023-24 is approximately 113-111 per team. The game occurred when neither team played meaningful defense, resulting in a historically unusual scoreline.
questions.memory_hook: Detroit 186, Denver 184 — a combined 370 points in one NBA game in 1983. For comparison, modern teams average around 113 per game. Both coaches essentially agreed to play no defense and see who scored more. It was basketball as pure offense, and it will almost certainly never be repeated.

questions.question: 'Dune' (1965) by Frank Herbert created one of science fiction's most detailed worlds. What resource does the desert planet Arrakis produce that makes it the most important planet in the universe?
options: Water (in a universe where freshwater is extinct everywhere else)
questions.explanation: The Spice Melange is a drug produced by sandworms on Arrakis that extends life, enhances mental abilities, and is essential for interstellar navigation (navigators consume it to fold space). Whoever controls Arrakis controls the universe's most valuable substance. Herbert conceived spice as an analogy for oil — a resource that creates geopolitical dependency. Dune was the first sci-fi novel to win the Hugo and Nebula Awards simultaneously and has sold ~20 million copies. Denis Villeneuve's film adaptations (2021, 2024) were critically acclaimed.
questions.memory_hook: Dune's spice is Herbert's oil allegory — a planet-specific resource that the entire civilization depends on, creating irresistible geopolitical pressure on whoever controls it. He wrote it in 1965 after the first OPEC crises; the parallel was intentional. The novel predicted resource politics before they became the defining issue of the late 20th century.

questions.question: Which singer released the best-selling album of all time, 'Thriller' (1982)?
questions.explanation: Michael Jackson's Thriller has sold an estimated 66-70 million copies worldwide — the best-selling album of all time. It produced 7 Top 10 singles from a single album (a record). The title track's music video (14 minutes, directed by John Landis) premiered on MTV in 1983 and was the first video to be inducted into the Library of Congress's National Film Registry. The album won 8 Grammy Awards in one night (another record at the time). Jackson's 'moonwalk' debuted on the Motown 25th Anniversary special while promoting Thriller.
questions.memory_hook: Thriller's 7 Top 10 singles from one album remains a record. Jackson's moonwalk debuted on TV during the Thriller era — a dance move he'd been developing for years, introduced at the perfect moment of peak global attention. The Thriller video is the most expensive music video ever made at the time.

questions.question: 'Hamlet' contains the soliloquy 'To be or not to be,' but which Shakespeare play contains the line 'All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players'?
questions.explanation: The 'seven ages of man' speech ('All the world's a stage...') is spoken by Jaques in Act 2, Scene 7 of As You Like It. The speech describes the seven stages of human life: infant, schoolboy, lover, soldier, justice, pantaloon, and finally 'second childishness and mere oblivion' in old age. As You Like It is a romantic comedy set in the Forest of Arden. Shakespeare's comedies are less frequently studied than his tragedies, but As You Like It, A Midsummer Night's Dream, and Twelfth Night are among his most performed works.
questions.memory_hook: Shakespeare's 'seven ages of man' describes life from infant to senility — and it's from a comedy, not a tragedy. The speech's vision of life ending in 'mere oblivion' gives As You Like It a darker edge than most romantic comedies. Shakespeare hid philosophy in his entertainments.

questions.question: The Great Rift Valley's lakes include some of the deepest and most species-rich in the world. Which East African lake is the world's longest freshwater lake?
questions.explanation: Lake Tanganyika is the world's longest freshwater lake (~676 km) and second-deepest (1,470m, after Lake Baikal). It borders Democratic Republic of Congo, Tanzania, Burundi, and Zambia. Lake Tanganyika is estimated to be 9-12 million years old and contains approximately 350 fish species, 98% of which are found nowhere else (endemic). These fish evolved in isolation, providing one of the most dramatic examples of adaptive radiation. Lake Victoria is the largest African lake by area but is much shallower.
questions.memory_hook: Lake Tanganyika is 676 km long but almost narrow enough to see across — it formed in the Rift Valley where Africa is tearing apart. Its 9-12 million years of isolation created 350 fish species found nowhere else. Darwin would have loved it: evolution's results visible in real time.

questions.question: The world's largest country by area is Russia. What percentage of Earth's land area does Russia cover?
questions.explanation: Russia covers approximately 17.1 million km² — about 11% of Earth's total land area (150 million km²) and spanning 11 time zones. It borders 14 countries (the most of any country) and touches three oceans (Arctic, Pacific, Atlantic via the Baltic). Despite its vast size, Russia's population is only ~144 million — with most concentrated in western European Russia. Siberia (the vast majority of Russia's territory) is home to only ~40 million people. Russia has the world's largest forest (the taiga boreal forest) and its largest reserves of natural gas.
questions.memory_hook: Russia is 11% of Earth's land — about twice the size of Antarctica. It spans 11 time zones: when it's midnight in Kaliningrad (near Poland), it's 10 AM the next day in Kamchatka. Despite this size, 75% of Russians live in the western 20% of the country, mostly west of the Ural Mountains.

questions.question: What is nuclear fusion, and why is it considered the 'holy grail' of energy production?
options: Nuclear fusion produces energy by splitting radioactive atoms; it's sought because it produces no heat and is perfectly safe
Nuclear fusion is the same as fission but done at lower temperatures; it's sought because it uses cheaper fuel

Nuclear fusion combines light atomic nuclei (like hydrogen isotopes) to form heavier nuclei, releasing enormous energy. It's sought because the fuel (hydrogen from seawater) is virtually inexhaustible, the reaction produces no CO₂, and waste products (helium, short-lived tritium) are far less dangerous than fission's radioactive waste
Nuclear fusion is a theoretical process that has never been demonstrated; its pursuit is speculative
questions.answer: Nuclear fusion combines light atomic nuclei (like hydrogen isotopes) to form heavier nuclei, releasing enormous energy. It's sought because the fuel (hydrogen from seawater) is virtually inexhaustible, the reaction produces no CO₂, and waste products (helium, short-lived tritium) are far less dangerous than fission's radioactive waste
questions.explanation: Fusion powers the Sun (hydrogen → helium). On Earth, the challenge is containing plasma at 100+ million degrees — hot enough for fusion. Two approaches: magnetic confinement (ITER project, tokamak design, under construction in France) and inertial confinement (laser-driven, demonstrated at NIF in December 2022 when they achieved fusion ignition — more energy out than laser energy in). Commercial fusion power remains years to decades away, but progress in the 2020s has been faster than any previous decade.
questions.memory_hook: In December 2022, NIF achieved fusion ignition — more energy out than laser energy in for the first time. This was a historic milestone, though 'net energy' fusion accounting for all input energy is still years away. The joke has been 'fusion is 20 years away and always will be' — but the 2020s have been the most promising decade yet.

questions.question: Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) — millisecond-duration pulses of radio energy from billions of light-years away — are one of astronomy's most energetic and mysterious phenomena. What is currently known about them?
options: FRBs have been confirmed as signals from artificial transmitters in nearby galaxies — the energy level and precise millisecond timing rule out all known natural astrophysical processes

FRBs release in milliseconds the energy our Sun produces in thousands of years; some repeat, some don't; the leading explanation is magnetars — neutron stars with extraordinarily powerful magnetic fields
FRBs originate exclusively from the Milky Way's galactic center and have been traced to a specific region within 100 light-years of Sagittarius A*, our central black hole
FRBs are caused by collisions between neutron stars — each burst corresponds to a merger event, making them reliable indicators of gravitational wave emission that should be detectable by LIGO
questions.answer: FRBs release in milliseconds the energy our Sun produces in thousands of years; some repeat, some don't; the leading explanation is magnetars — neutron stars with extraordinarily powerful magnetic fields
questions.explanation: The first FRB was discovered in 2007 in archived data from the Parkes Telescope in Australia. Since then hundreds have been detected, some repeating (like FRB 121102), some apparently one-off events. In 2020, an FRB was detected from within the Milky Way — traced to a known magnetar (SGR 1935+2154) — providing the strongest evidence yet that magnetars are responsible. Magnetars have magnetic fields a trillion times stronger than Earth's, and eruptions in their magnetic crusts could produce the observed radio blasts. Most FRBs come from billions of light-years away, making the energy output almost incomprehensibly large.
questions.memory_hook: An FRB lasts milliseconds but releases more energy than the Sun produces in thousands of years — from billions of light-years away. In 2020, one was detected inside our galaxy and traced to a magnetar (a neutron star with a magnetic field a trillion times Earth's) — the most likely culprit for all FRBs.
